title: Kubernetes for Linux Engineers
description: Kubernetes is a Linux cluster operating system. Every Kubernetes node runs Linux, and every container is a Linux process. Understanding Kubernetes from the Linux perspective — kubelet, CRI, CNI, CSI, cgroups, namespaces, eBPF — transforms how you debug cluster issues and design resilient systems. At Enterprise, this Linux-first understanding is what separates infrastructure engineers who 'manage YAML' from those who 'operate clusters'.

sections.title: Kubelet: The Node Agent
sections.description: The kubelet is the primary node agent — it registers the node with the control plane, watches for pod assignments, and ensures containers are running. It interacts with the container runtime via CRI (Container Runtime Interface). At Enterprise, the kubelet is configured with custom flags for node-level resource reservation, pod eviction thresholds, and garbage collection — all critical for cluster stability.
sections.codeBlock:
# === Kubelet on the node ===
ps aux | grep kubelet                  # Check kubelet process and flags

# Key kubelet flags:
# --node-ip=10.0.0.5                    # Node's internal IP
# --container-runtime-endpoint=unix:///run/containerd/containerd.sock
# --kubeconfig=/var/lib/kubelet/kubeconfig
# --register-node=true
# --node-status-update-frequency=10s
# --eviction-hard=memory.available<100Mi,nodefs.available<10%
# --system-reserved=cpu=200m,memory=512Mi  # Resources reserved for system
# --kube-reserved=cpu=200m,memory=512Mi    # Resources reserved for kubelet itself

# === Kubelet logs ===
journalctl -u kubelet -n 100 --no-pager  # Recent kubelet logs
journalctl -u kubelet -f                 # Follow kubelet logs

# === Kubelet state directory ===
ls /var/lib/kubelet/
# pod-manifests/        — Static pod YAML files
# device-plugins/       — GPU/device plugin sockets
# pods/                 — Pod sandbox directories (one per pod)
# cpu_manager_state     — CPU manager state (static CPU pinning)

# === Pod sandbox on disk ===
ls /var/lib/kubelet/pods/<POD_UID>/
# containers/           — container logs and state
# volumes/              — mounted volumes
# etc-hosts             — pod-level /etc/hosts

# === Check node capacity ===
kubectl describe node <node> | grep -A 10 "Capacity"
# cpu, memory, pods, ephemeral-storage, hugepages
# Also: nvidia.com/gpu, nvidia.com/mig-1g.10gb (device plugin resources)

# === Kubelet garbage collection ===
# Image GC: remove unused images when disk > threshold
# Container GC: remove terminated containers
# Eviction: kill pods when node resources are low
# Kubelet flags:
# --image-gc-high-threshold=85         # Start GC when disk > 85%
# --image-gc-low-threshold=80          # Stop GC when disk < 80%
# --maximum-dead-containers-per-container=5

sections.title: CRI: Container Runtime Interface
sections.description: The CRI abstracts the container runtime behind a gRPC API. containerd is the default runtime (used by Docker, EKS, GKE, AKS). cri-o is a Kubernetes-native runtime optimized for CRI. runc is the low-level OCI runtime. At Enterprise, containerd is standard — but some workloads use alternative runtimes for security (gVisor, Kata Containers) or performance (crun in Rust).
sections.codeBlock:
# === Check container runtime ===
kubectl get nodes -o wide | awk '{print $1, $NF}'
# Shows container-runtime version for each node

# === containerd (most common) ===
# containerd socket:
ls /run/containerd/containerd.sock

# containerd commands:
ctr images list                       # List images in containerd
ctr containers list                   # List containers
ctr tasks list                        # List running tasks
ctr image pull docker.io/library/nginx:latest

# === crictl (CRI debug tool — ESSENTIAL) ===
crictl pods                           # List pods (CRI-level)
crictl ps                             # List containers
crictl images                         # List images
crictl inspect <CONTAINER_ID>         # Detailed container info
crictl logs <CONTAINER_ID>            # Container logs (bypasses kubectl)
crictl exec -it <CONTAINER_ID> sh    # Exec into container
crictl stats                          # Resource usage (CPU, memory)

# === crictl vs docker ===
# crictl is the CRI-native replacement for docker CLI
# Use crictl when docker CLI is not available (containerd-only nodes)
# Same concepts: pods=, containers=, images=

# === Low-level OCI runtime ===
# runc: the reference OCI runtime
# Creates cgroups + namespaces + mounts for each container
# Check which runtime:
runc --version

# === Runtime alternatives ===
# crun (Rust) — faster than runc, used by Red Hat/OpenShift
# gVisor (Google) — user-space kernel, stronger isolation than runc
# Kata Containers — lightweight VM per container, hardware isolation
# Check node:
kubectl get nodes -o json | jq '.items[].status.nodeInfo.containerRuntimeVersion'

# === CRI-O ===
# Red Hat's Kubernetes-native runtime
# Socket: /run/crio/crio.sock
# Use crictl with: crictl --runtime-endpoint unix:///run/crio/crio.sock

sections.title: CNI: Container Networking Interface
sections.description: CNI is the plugin model for Kubernetes networking. Each pod gets its own network namespace. The CNI plugin creates a veth pair, attaches one end to the pod namespace and the other to a bridge/overlay, and assigns an IP address. At Enterprise, choices include: Cilium (eBPF-based, fastest growing), Calico (policy-rich, widely used), and AWS VPC CNI (native VPC IPs, no overlay).
sections.codeBlock:
# === Pod networking (Linux view) ===
kubectl exec <pod> -- ip addr        # Inside pod: network namespace
kubectl exec <pod> -- ip route       # Inside pod: routing table
kubectl exec <pod> -- cat /etc/resolv.conf  # DNS config (inside pod)

# === Node-side: pod network namespace ===
# Find pod's network namespace:
POD_UID=$(kubectl get pod <pod> -o jsonpath='{.metadata.uid}')
# Enter pod's netns from node:
ln -sf /proc/1/ns/net /var/run/netns/default  # (on some systems)
# Better: use nsenter

# === CNI plugins directory ===
ls /opt/cni/bin/                      # Installed CNI plugins
# bridge, host-local, loopback, portmap, bandwidth, tuning

# === CNI config ===
cat /etc/cni/net.d/10-aws.conflist    # CNI configuration (varies by plugin)

# === Check which CNI is in use ===
kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep -E "(cilium|calico|flannel|weave|aws-node)"

# === Cilium (eBPF-based CNI) ===
# Cilium replaces kube-proxy entirely with eBPF
# No iptables rules needed — eBPF programs handle service load balancing
# Check Cilium status:
kubectl -n kube-system exec daemonset/cilium -- cilium status
kubectl -n kube-system exec daemonset/cilium -- cilium endpoint list

# === Cilium Hubble (network observability) ===
# Shows pod-to-pod traffic with L3/L4/L7 visibility
# Requires no application changes — eBPF captures all traffic

# === Network policy ===
# CNI plugins enforce Kubernetes NetworkPolicy
# (default: all pods can communicate — you must add policies)
kubectl get networkpolicies --all-namespaces

sections.title: CSI: Container Storage Interface
sections.description: CSI provides a standard API for storage providers (EBS, EFS, GCE PD, Azure Disk). When a pod requests a PVC, the CSI driver provisions the underlying storage (e.g., an EBS volume), attaches it to the node, mounts it into the pod's filesystem. At Enterprise, understanding CSI is essential for debugging volume mount failures, performance issues, and capacity planning.
sections.codeBlock:
# === Pod storage (Linux view) ===
kubectl exec <pod> -- df -h            # Mounted volumes inside pod
kubectl exec <pod> -- mount            # All mounts inside pod
kubectl exec <pod> -- ls -la /dev/     # Block devices inside pod

# === Node-side: volume mounts ===
# Host path for mounted volumes:
ls /var/lib/kubelet/pods/<POD_UID>/volumes/kubernetes.io~csi/
# Or for in-tree volumes:
ls /var/lib/kubelet/pods/<POD_UID>/volumes/kubernetes.io~aws-ebs/

# === Check CSI driver ===
kubectl get csidrivers                  # Installed CSI drivers
kubectl get nodes -o json | jq '.items[0].status.volumesAttached'
# Shows which volumes are attached to the node

# === EBS CSI driver debugging ===
kubectl logs -n kube-system -l app=ebs-csi-node  # Node-side driver logs

# Find EBS volume ID for a PVC:
PV_NAME=$(kubectl get pvc <pvc> -o jsonpath='{.spec.volumeName}')
VOLUME_ID=$(kubectl get pv $PV_NAME -o jsonpath='{.spec.csi.volumeHandle}')
echo $VOLUME_ID  # This is the EBS volume ID

# === Node-level: check attached volumes ===
lsblk                                 # Check if EBS volume is attached
sudo nvme list                        # NVMe device list (Nitro instances)
# Modern AWS Nitro instances expose EBS as NVMe devices

# === Filesystem inside volume ===
# If a volume can't mount, check:
# 1. Is the volume attached? (lsblk)
# 2. Is there a filesystem? (file -s /dev/nvme1n1)
# 3. Can it be mounted manually? (mount /dev/nvme1n1 /mnt)

# === Storage classes ===
kubectl get storageclass
# Each SC defines: provisioner, reclaim policy, filesystem type, IOPS, etc.
# Example: gp3 EBS with 5000 IOPS

sections.title: Interview Q&A
sections.description:
Q: 'A pod is stuck in 'ContainerCreating' state. How do you debug from the Linux node perspective?'

A: 'SSH into the node (or use kubectl node shell). Check kubelet logs: journalctl -u kubelet --since '5 min ago'. Common causes: (1) Image pull failure — check network connectivity to the registry. (2) Volume mount failure — check if the CSI driver created the volume, if it's attached (lsblk), if the filesystem exists. (3) Resource pressure — the node may be out of CPU/memory/disk. Run kubectl describe node for conditions. (4) CNI error — pod network setup failed. Check CNI daemon logs. (5) Admission webhook timeout — sometimes a webhook hangs and prevents pod creation. At Enterprise, we have automation that detects 'ContainerCreating' > 2 minutes and pages the on-call engineer with the most likely cause based on patterns.'

Q: 'How does kube-proxy work, and why is Cilium replacing it?'

A: 'Kube-proxy implements Service load balancing via userspace proxy, iptables, or IPVS. The iptables mode creates iptables rules for every Service endpoint — on a 10,000-service cluster, this means 100K+ iptables rules. Every packet traverses all these rules (O(n) cost), causing latency spikes during rule updates. Cilium replaces this with eBPF — a BPF program for each Service that runs at the XDP or TC layer, with O(1) lookup. Updates are atomic (bpf map update, no rule churn). Result: lower latency, better scalability, and no iptables overhead. At Enterprise, any cluster with > 1000 services has either moved to Cilium or IPVS.'

Q: 'A node is reporting 'DiskPressure'. What's happening at the Linux level?'

A: 'DiskPressure means the node's available disk space or inodes are below the kubelet's eviction threshold. Check df -h and df -i on the node. Common culprits: (1) Container logs not rotated — /var/log/pods/ can grow large. (2) Docker/containerd images — docker system prune -a or crictl rmi --prune. (3) Ephemeral volume usage — pods writing to emptyDir volumes. (4) System logs — journalctl --vacuum-size=500M. Kubelet will start evicting pods when DiskPressure is true, starting with the lowest priority pods. At Enterprise, we set aggressive kubelet GC thresholds and monitor node disk usage to prevent this.'

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• Kubernetes Architecture — Official docs on nodes, kubelet, control plane
  • CRI Specification — Container Runtime Interface deep dive
  • Cilium: eBPF for Kubernetes — How Cilium replaces kube-proxy with eBPF

analogy.title: Kubernetes as a Port Authority
analogy.description: Pods are containers sharing a dock. Services are stable addresses for shifting pods. Deployments manage rolling updates. The scheduler assigns pods to nodes (ships). Controllers (replicasets) maintain desired state. etcd is the manifest - the source of truth.

commonMistakes.mistake: Ignoring log rotation until disk fills up
commonMistakes.solution: Configure logrotate before deploying; monitor /var/log usage

commonMistakes.mistake: Disabling SELinux/AppArmor instead of fixing policies
commonMistakes.solution: Use `audit2allow` to generate policies from denials; keep MAC enabled
Add `USER nonroot` in Dockerfile; use `--user` flag at runtime

commonMistakes.mistake: Not setting resource limits on containers
commonMistakes.solution: Always set `--cpus` and `--memory`; configure cgroup limits in Kubernetes

practiceLabs.title: Explore: Kubelet: The Node Agent
practiceLabs.task: Run `ps` and examine the output. Note the structure, key fields, and patterns you observe. Try different flags to see how the output changes.
practiceLabs.expectedOutput: Output will vary by system. Look for structured fields, key identifiers, and configuration sections.
practiceLabs.hint: Use `man ps` to see all available flags and understand the output columns.

practiceLabs.title: Explore: CRI: Container Runtime Interface
practiceLabs.task: Run `journalctl` and examine the output. Note the structure, key fields, and patterns you observe. Try different flags to see how the output changes.
practiceLabs.expectedOutput: Output will vary by system. Look for structured fields, key identifiers, and configuration sections.
practiceLabs.hint: Use `man journalctl` to see all available flags and understand the output columns.

practiceLabs.title: Explore: CNI: Container Networking Interface
practiceLabs.task: Run `ls` and examine the output. Note the structure, key fields, and patterns you observe. Try different flags to see how the output changes.
practiceLabs.expectedOutput: Output will vary by system. Look for structured fields, key identifiers, and configuration sections.
practiceLabs.hint: Use `man ls` to see all available flags and understand the output columns.

quickReference.title: Quick Reference
quickReference.content:
| Command | Description |
|---|---|
| `ps` | Kubelet: The Node Agent |
| `journalctl` | Kubelet: The Node Agent |
| `ls` | Kubelet: The Node Agent |
| `kubectl` | CRI: Container Runtime Interface |
| `ls` | CRI: Container Runtime Interface |
| `ctr` | CRI: Container Runtime Interface |
| `kubectl` | CNI: Container Networking Interface |
| `POD_UID` | CNI: Container Networking Interface |
| `ln` | CNI: Container Networking Interface |
| `kubectl` | CSI: Container Storage Interface |
| `ls` | CSI: Container Storage Interface |
| `PV_NAME` | CSI: Container Storage Interface |

troubleshooting.cause: Incorrect arguments, wrong working directory, or stale cached data
troubleshooting.solution: Verify arguments with `man ps`; check current path with `pwd`; clear cache if applicable

troubleshooting.cause: Service not reloaded or syntax error in config file
troubleshooting.solution: Validate config syntax with built-in test flag; reload service with `systemctl reload` or `restart`

troubleshooting.symptom: kubernetes operation slow or timing out
troubleshooting.cause: Resource exhaustion (disk I/O, memory pressure, or lock contention)
troubleshooting.solution: Check `iostat`, `free -m`, and `top`; look for stuck processes with `ps aux --sort=-%cpu`

troubleshooting.cause: Incorrect file ownership or overly restrictive permissions
troubleshooting.solution: Inspect with `ls -la`; fix with `chown`/ `chmod`; check ACLs with `getfacl`

quiz.question: What is Kubelet: The Node Agent?
options: A standardized directory structure that defines where files and directories should be placed on Linux systems
A tool for managing disk partitions
quiz.answer: A standardized directory structure that defines where files and directories should be placed on Linux systems
quiz.explanation: The Kubelet: The Node Agent is the foundation of the Linux filesystem — everything in the system is organized under this structure.

quiz.question: What does `ps` do?
quiz.answer: Lists files and directories in the current or specified path
quiz.explanation: Use `ls` to explore directory contents. Combine with flags like `-l` (long format), `-a` (hidden files), and `-h` (human-readable sizes).

quiz.question: Which command would you use to view disk usage of the kubernetes directory?
quiz.explanation: `du -sh` (disk usage, summary, human-readable) shows the total size of a directory and its contents.

quiz.question: A user reports "Permission denied" when trying to access a kubernetes directory. What is the first command you should run?
quiz.explanation: `ls -ld` shows the directory's permissions and ownership. Always inspect before making changes — starting with `chmod 777` is a security anti-pattern.

quiz.question: In a kubernetes scenario, your monitoring alerts show ps failing intermittently. What is the correct debugging workflow?
options: Check logs → verify configuration → test in staging → apply fix → monitor
Ignore the alert and wait for it to resolve
quiz.answer: Check logs → verify configuration → test in staging → apply fix → monitor
quiz.explanation: A structured debugging workflow prevents cascading failures. Always diagnose before acting, especially in production environments where downtime has direct business impact.

interviewTips: FAANG Architect's Note:  Kubernetes is often taught as a YAML abstraction — you write manifests, you get clusters
productionTips: Production Tip:  Every Kubernetes engineer should know crictl
bestPractices: Use version control for all configuration (/etc, scripts, Dockerfiles)
Implement infrastructure as code; avoid manual server changes
Test changes in staging before production; have rollback plans
enterprisePerspective: In enterprise environments, kubernetes for linux engineers is managed through automation (Ansible, Terraform), monitored centrally (Prometheus, Datadog), and governed by compliance policies (CIS benchmarks, SOC2). Changes go through change advisory boards with rollback procedures documented.
